questions.scenario: A developer has `Reader` at the subscription scope and `Contributor` on `rg-devpolaris-orders-dev`. They cannot update resources in `rg-devpolaris-orders-prod`. What explains this?

options.text: The broader subscription Reader role does not grant write access, and the Contributor role is scoped only to the dev resource group.
Contributor on the dev resource group may be enough if the prod group inherits the same subscription Reader role.
Reader at subscription scope blocks all lower-level role assignments from working.
The developer may need to use the portal because some production role assignments are not honored by CLI tools.

questions.explanation: Azure RBAC is scoped. A role assignment on one resource group does not write to another. A broader Reader role gives read access, not production write access.

questions.scenario: A policy is assigned at the subscription scope requiring the tag `cost-center`. A deployment to a new resource group fails because the tag is missing. Why did a resource group deployment hit a subscription policy?

options.text: Policy can inherit from broader scopes, so a subscription-level rule can apply to resource groups and resources inside it.
Resource groups ignore policy, so the failure must be from the app code.
Tags may be checked by Cost Management later, but this specific failure should be handled in the cost workflow instead of deployment.
The region may have a local tagging rule, so the team should inspect regional settings before policy scope.

questions.explanation: Scope inheritance is the key concept. A subscription policy can apply to child resource groups and resources. This is not an app-code failure.

questions.scenario: Maya is designing access for a release pipeline that deploys only `devpolaris-orders-api` into `rg-devpolaris-orders-prod`. Which choices keep the blast radius smaller?

options.text: Assign only the roles the pipeline needs instead of broad Owner access.
Scope the assignment to the resource group or specific resources when that is enough.
Use a dedicated service principal or managed identity for the pipeline instead of a personal user login.
Assign subscription Owner because it is easier to debug and can be cleaned up later.

questions.explanation: Least privilege is practical here: choose a dedicated identity, narrow role, and narrow scope. Subscription Owner may make the first deploy easier, but it creates unnecessary blast radius.

questions.scenario: The production team can create resources, but every attempt to create a public IP is denied. The error mentions a policy assignment at `/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/devpolaris-prod`. What does this tell you?

options.text: A management group policy is being inherited by the subscription, so the team should inspect policy before changing resource-level settings.
The public IP SKU might be unavailable in the chosen region, so service availability should be checked before policy.
The resource group may be missing the tag that allows public IP creation under the team's naming convention.
A narrower resource-level role assignment may give the deployer enough permission to bypass the inherited deny.

questions.explanation: The scope in the error points above the subscription. Management group policy can control child subscriptions. RBAC changes at a single resource do not bypass that policy.

questions.scenario: A teammate wants to assign `Key Vault Secrets Officer` at the whole production subscription so one API can read one secret. The API has its own managed identity, and the secret lives in a known production vault. What is the better first design?

options.text: Use a dedicated app identity and grant the smallest useful secret access at the Key Vault or secret scope supported by the team's model.
Assign the role at subscription scope because secret names are hard to predict during runtime.
Give the on-call group the same role temporarily so incidents are easier while the API identity is tested.
Store the secret in the resource group tags so RBAC is no longer needed.

questions.explanation: The access need is narrow: one app reading one secret or one vault. A broad subscription assignment is tempting because it is simple, but it grants much more than the app needs.
